infobox.value: [Allegany](/wiki/Allegany_County,_New_York "Allegany County, New York"), [Broome](/wiki/Broome_County,_New_York "Broome County, New York"), [Cattaraugus](/wiki/Cattaraugus_County "Cattaraugus County"), [Chautauqua](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York"), [Chemung](/wiki/Chemung_County,_New_York "Chemung County, New York"), [Chenango](/wiki/Chenango_County,_New_York "Chenango County, New York"), [Cortland](/wiki/Cortland_County "Cortland County"), [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_County,_New_York "Delaware County, New York"), [Otsego](/wiki/Otsego_County,_New_York "Otsego County, New York"), [Schoharie](/wiki/Schoharie_County "Schoharie County"), [Schuyler](/wiki/Schuyler_County,_New_York "Schuyler County, New York"), [Steuben](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York"), [Tioga](/wiki/Tioga_County,_New_York "Tioga County, New York"), [Tompkins](/wiki/Tompkins_County,_New_York "Tompkins County, New York")

[Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York"), [Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York"), [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York"), [Hornell](/wiki/Hornell,_New_York "Hornell, New York"), [Ithaca](/wiki/Ithaca,_New_York "Ithaca, New York"), [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York"), [Olean](/wiki/Olean,_New_York "Olean, New York")

is a geographic subregion of the broader [upstate](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") region of [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), geographically situated along or very near the state border with [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). Definitions of the region vary widely, but generally encompass counties surrounding the [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York"), [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York")-[Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York"), and [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area"), along with the land of the [Seneca Nation](/wiki/Seneca_Nation "Seneca Nation"). This region is adjacent to the [Northern Tier](/wiki/Northern_Tier_(Pennsylvania) "Northern Tier (Pennsylvania)") of Pennsylvania, and both these regions together are known as the [Twin Tiers](/wiki/Twin_Tiers "Twin Tiers").

| * [v](/wiki/Template:Regions_of_New_York "Template:Regions of New York") * [t](/wiki/Template_talk:Regions_of_New_York "Template talk:Regions of New York") * [e](/wiki/Special:EditPage/Template:Regions_of_New_York "Special:EditPage/Template:Regions of New York") |

The **Southern Tier** is a geographic subregion of the broader [upstate](/wiki/Upstate_New_York "Upstate New York") region of [New York State](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), geographically situated along or very near the state border with [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). Definitions of the region vary widely, but generally encompass counties surrounding the [Binghamton](/wiki/Binghamton,_New_York "Binghamton, New York"), [Elmira](/wiki/Elmira,_New_York "Elmira, New York")-[Corning](/wiki/Corning_(city),_New_York "Corning (city), New York"), and [Jamestown](/wiki/Jamestown,_New_York "Jamestown, New York") [metropolitan areas](/wiki/Metropolitan_area "Metropolitan area"), along with the land of the [Seneca Nation](/wiki/Seneca_Nation "Seneca Nation"). This region is adjacent to the [Northern Tier](/wiki/Northern_Tier_(Pennsylvania) "Northern Tier (Pennsylvania)") of Pennsylvania, and both these regions together are known as the [Twin Tiers](/wiki/Twin_Tiers "Twin Tiers").

Geographically, most of the Southern Tier resides in the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau") of the [Appalachian Mountains](/wiki/Appalachian_Mountains "Appalachian Mountains"), with the eastern areas of the region nestled in the western portion of the [Catskill Mountains](/wiki/Catskill_Mountains "Catskill Mountains"). A longtime home of the [Iroquois Confederacy](/wiki/Iroquois_Confederacy "Iroquois Confederacy"), European settlers moved to the region after the [American Revolutionary War](/wiki/American_Revolutionary_War "American Revolutionary War"). The fertile yet hilly land, combined with sweeping river valleys, led the region to support a combination of manufacturing industries (including large companies such as [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") and [Corning Inc.](/wiki/Corning_Inc. "Corning Inc.")) and farming, but with less development compared to neighboring subregions of upstate. Since the 1950s, the area is often considered to be a part of the [Rust Belt](/wiki/Rust_Belt "Rust Belt"), as manufacturing jobs have left the region.

## Definition and constituent counties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Definition and constituent counties")]

As is the case with many regions in New York State, there is no legal definition of the Southern Tier, resulting a lack of consensus about which counties belong to the region. One of the broadest definitions of the Southern Tier is from the [New York State Department of State](/wiki/New_York_State_Department_of_State "New York State Department of State"), which classifies the following 14 counties as members of the Southern Tier:[[1]](#cite_note-1)

* [Allegany County](/wiki/Allegany_County,_New_York "Allegany County, New York")
* [Broome County](/wiki/Broome_County "Broome County")
* [Cattaraugus County](/wiki/Cattaraugus_County "Cattaraugus County")
* [Chautauqua County](/wiki/Chautauqua_County,_New_York "Chautauqua County, New York")
* [Chemung County](/wiki/Chemung_County "Chemung County")
* [Chenango County](/wiki/Chenango_County "Chenango County")
* [Cortland County](/wiki/Cortland_County "Cortland County")
* [Delaware County](/wiki/Delaware_County,_New_York "Delaware County, New York")
* [Otsego County](/wiki/Otsego_County,_New_York "Otsego County, New York")
* [Schoharie County](/wiki/Schoharie_County "Schoharie County")
* [Schuyler County](/wiki/Schuyler_County,_New_York "Schuyler County, New York")
* [Steuben County](/wiki/Steuben_County,_New_York "Steuben County, New York")
* [Tioga County](/wiki/Tioga_County,_New_York "Tioga County, New York")
* [Tompkins County](/wiki/Tompkins_County "Tompkins County")

This definition corresponds to the same 14 counties in New York State that are members of the [Appalachian Regional Commission](/wiki/Appalachian_Regional_Commission "Appalachian Regional Commission").
Based on this definition, the Southern Tier partially overlaps with the [Western New York](/wiki/Western_New_York "Western New York"), [Finger Lakes](/wiki/Finger_Lakes "Finger Lakes"), and [Mohawk Valley](/wiki/Mohawk_Valley "Mohawk Valley") regions.
Less expansive definitions can vary widely, such as the eight-county region defined by the state's [Empire State Development Corporation](/wiki/Empire_State_Development_Corporation "Empire State Development Corporation") or the four-county region in the Encyclopedia of New York State.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Much of the Southern Tier is in [area code 607](/wiki/Area_code_607 "Area code 607"), with the exception of Allegany, Cattaraugus, and Chautauqua Counties, which are in [area code 716](/wiki/Area_code_716 "Area code 716") or [area code 585](/wiki/Area_code_585 "Area code 585"). As of 2023, the westernmost portion of the Southern Tier is located in [New York's 23rd congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_23rd_congressional_district "New York's 23rd congressional district"), and the easternmost portion is located in [New York's 19th congressional district](/wiki/New_York%27s_19th_congressional_district "New York's 19th congressional district"). The ZIP code prefixes 137 – 139 (Binghamton region), 147 (Jamestown region), and 148 – 149 (Elmira region) are set aside for the Southern Tier.

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Geography")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5b/Allegany_State_Park_Mt._Tuscarora_2007.jpg/250px-Allegany_State_Park_Mt._Tuscarora_2007.jpg)](/wiki/File:Allegany_State_Park_Mt._Tuscarora_2007.jpg)

Allegany State Park

The Southern Tier is generally hilly without being mountainous (with the exception of the Catskill mountains). This can range from low rolling hills to more steep and rugged cliffs and valleys. The highest point in western New York is [Alma Hill](/wiki/Alma_Hill "Alma Hill") in [Allegany County](/wiki/Allegany_County,_New_York "Allegany County, New York") near the Steuben County line in the Southern Tier. Both the [Delaware](/wiki/Delaware_River "Delaware River") and [Susquehanna](/wiki/Susquehanna_River "Susquehanna River") rivers flow through the Southern Tier in their upper reaches, as does the [Allegheny River](/wiki/Allegheny_River "Allegheny River") in the western Southern Tier.

The Southern Tier makes up the northernmost portion of [Appalachia](/wiki/Appalachia "Appalachia") and lies on the [Allegheny Plateau](/wiki/Allegheny_Plateau "Allegheny Plateau"). It is defined on its western boundary by the Chautauqua Ridge in Chautauqua County, and including this ridge and extending eastward across the northern bounds of the region, the continental divide between the [Great Lakes](/wiki/Great_Lakes "Great Lakes") and the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River") watersheds exists. The [Eastern Continental Divide](/wiki/Eastern_Continental_Divide "Eastern Continental Divide") runs directly through the region, in Steuben County.

The Southern Tier is more geographically linked with Pennsylvania and the Susquehanna River valley, at its confluence with the [Chemung River](/wiki/Chemung_River "Chemung River"), than it is with the remainder of New York State. Historically, the population of the Southern Tier came primarily from Pennsylvania, New Jersey, and [downstate New York](/wiki/Downstate_New_York "Downstate New York") ("Yorkers"). In contrast, the inhabitants of upper New York State came from New England ("Yankees").[[3]](#cite_note-Sernett-3): 11

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: History")]

The Southern Tier has long been home to the people of the [Iroquois](/wiki/Iroquois "Iroquois") Confederacy. There were major settlements along the Allegheny River in Cattaraugus County (which the Senecas acquired by defeating the [Wenrohronon](/wiki/Wenrohronon "Wenrohronon") during the [Beaver Wars](/wiki/Beaver_Wars "Beaver Wars") in 1638) and at [Painted Post](/wiki/Painted_Post "Painted Post") in Steuben County, at what is today the northeast side of Corning, New York. The [Seneca Nation](/wiki/Seneca_Nation "Seneca Nation") has a reservation today along the Allegheny River and a headquarters at [Salamanca](/wiki/Salamanca_(city),_New_York "Salamanca (city), New York"). There are also native lands (with no current native residents) on [Cuba Lake](/wiki/Cuba_Lake "Cuba Lake") in Allegany County.

The colonies that eventually became the states of New York, [Massachusetts](/wiki/Massachusetts "Massachusetts") and [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania") all laid claim to the Southern Tier at various points in the 17th and 18th centuries, while not making any significant attempt to settle the territory.

The region was quickly settled by European descendants after the Revolutionary War, when settlers were again allowed west of the Appalachian divide. The Southern Tier shared in the economic growth of the early 19th century, but its hilly terrain made it less suitable to canal-building, and later, railroading, than the more-level corridor to the north between Albany and Buffalo. There was an attempt at a [Genesee Valley Canal](/wiki/Genesee_Valley_Canal "Genesee Valley Canal") in the western half, and in the eastern half, the [Chemung](/wiki/Chemung_Canal "Chemung Canal") and [Chenango](/wiki/Chenango_Canal "Chenango Canal") Canals did connect the [Erie Canal](/wiki/Erie_Canal "Erie Canal") to Elmira and Binghamton respectively. Beset by financial and technical difficulties, the latter two canals nonetheless were important catalysts for economic growth, and indeed for the construction of the railroads that would supplant them. Plans to connect these canals to the [Pennsylvania Canal](/wiki/Pennsylvania_Canal "Pennsylvania Canal") system, thus making them much more than feeders to the Erie Canal, never came to fruition.

Railroads did arrive and the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad"), which followed the water-level of the Allegheny, Susquehanna and Delaware watersheds, accelerated industrial progress in the region about the time of the [American Civil War](/wiki/American_Civil_War "American Civil War"). The railroad and available fuel from the region's dense forests attracted Corning Glass Works to Steuben County in 1868.

The region became home to prosperous farms and small factory towns (with the exception of larger Binghamton) during the first half of the 20th century. But declines in U.S. manufacturing hit the region hard and it suffered even more than other parts of upstate [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)") and northern [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania").

The region's addition to the [Appalachian Regional Commission](/wiki/Appalachian_Regional_Commission "Appalachian Regional Commission"), often credited to the influence of U.S. Sen. [Robert F. Kennedy](/wiki/Robert_F._Kennedy "Robert F. Kennedy"), provided economic stimulus over the last forty years. Government funds built the Southern Tier Expressway and highway links to the [New York State Thruway](/wiki/New_York_State_Thruway "New York State Thruway"), encouraged the growth of state colleges at Wellsville, Alfred and Binghamton and sought with mixed success to attract business interests relocating from the [New York Metropolitan Area](/wiki/New_York_Metropolitan_Area "New York Metropolitan Area") and urban Western New York.

For two decades, the region has tried to remake itself as a tourist destination and relocation area for retirees from big Northeastern cities. Meanwhile, agriculture and manufacturing struggle to compete regionally and globally.

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Education")]

[Binghamton University](/wiki/Binghamton_University "Binghamton University") (the State University of New York-Binghamton) is one of the [SUNY](/wiki/SUNY "SUNY") system's four [University Centers](/wiki/SUNY#University_centers "SUNY"). Other 4-year and graduate institutions within the core counties include [St. Bonaventure University](/wiki/St._Bonaventure_University "St. Bonaventure University"), [Alfred University](/wiki/Alfred_University "Alfred University"), [Elmira College](/wiki/Elmira_College "Elmira College"), and [Houghton College](/wiki/Houghton_College "Houghton College"). Technical and community colleges include [Alfred State College](/wiki/Alfred_State_College "Alfred State College"), [Broome Community College](/wiki/Broome_Community_College "Broome Community College"), [Corning Community College](/wiki/Corning_Community_College "Corning Community College"), and the [State University of New York at Delhi](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_at_Delhi "State University of New York at Delhi"). The region is also home to E.B.I. Career College, a [vocational school](/wiki/Vocational_school "Vocational school").

Institutions of higher learning outside the core counties include [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), [Hartwick College](/wiki/Hartwick_College "Hartwick College"), [Ithaca College](/wiki/Ithaca_College "Ithaca College"), SUNY Colleges in [Cortland](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_College_at_Cortland "State University of New York College at Cortland") and [Oneonta](/wiki/SUNY_College_at_Oneonta "SUNY College at Oneonta"), [Jamestown Community College](/wiki/Jamestown_Community_College "Jamestown Community College"), [Fredonia](/wiki/State_University_of_New_York_at_Fredonia "State University of New York at Fredonia"), and [Tompkins Cortland Community College](/wiki/Tompkins_Cortland_Community_College "Tompkins Cortland Community College").

## Transportation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Transportation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c8/Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg/250px-Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Southern_Tier_Expressway.svg)

Southern Tier Expressway route marker

The [Southern Tier Expressway](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Expressway "Southern Tier Expressway")—[Interstate 86](/wiki/Interstate_86_(Pennsylvania%E2%80%93New_York) "Interstate 86 (Pennsylvania–New York)") and [New York State Route 17](/wiki/New_York_State_Route_17 "New York State Route 17") together—serves the Southern Tier. The highway is the region's major corridor and connects to [U.S. Route 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219 "U.S. Route 219") in Salamanca, [Interstate 390](/wiki/Interstate_390_(New_York) "Interstate 390 (New York)") in Bath, [Interstate 99](/wiki/Interstate_99 "Interstate 99") in Corning, [U.S. Route 220](/wiki/U.S._Route_220 "U.S. Route 220") in Waverly, and [Interstates 81](/wiki/Interstate_81_in_New_York "Interstate 81 in New York") and [88](/wiki/Interstate_88_(New_York) "Interstate 88 (New York)") in Binghamton.

The region is served by three [regional airports](/wiki/Regional_airport "Regional airport"). [Elmira-Corning Regional Airport](/wiki/Elmira-Corning_Regional_Airport "Elmira-Corning Regional Airport") has routes to [Atlanta](/wiki/Atlanta_International_Airport "Atlanta International Airport"), [Washington D.C.](/wiki/Washington-Dulles_International_Airport "Washington-Dulles International Airport"), [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit_International_Airport "Detroit International Airport"), and several destinations in [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida"). The [Greater Binghamton Airport](/wiki/Greater_Binghamton_Airport "Greater Binghamton Airport") offers [Delta Air Lines](/wiki/Delta_Air_Lines "Delta Air Lines") flights to Detroit. [Chautauqua County-Jamestown Airport](/wiki/Chautauqua_County-Jamestown_Airport "Chautauqua County-Jamestown Airport") connects to [Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh,_Pennsylvania "Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania") via [Essential Air Service](/wiki/Essential_Air_Service "Essential Air Service").

Bus service is provided along the entire I-86/NY 17 corridor by [Coach USA](/wiki/Coach_USA "Coach USA")'s [Shortline](/wiki/Short_Line_(bus_company) "Short Line (bus company)")/Erie services from Jamestown to New York City and [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), and [Trailways](/wiki/Trailways "Trailways") connects the Southern Tier with [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"), [Dubois](/wiki/Dubois,_Pennsylvania "Dubois, Pennsylvania") (at the western end in Salamanca), [Sunbury](/wiki/Sunbury,_Pennsylvania "Sunbury, Pennsylvania")/[Lock Haven](/wiki/Lock_Haven,_Pennsylvania "Lock Haven, Pennsylvania") (at Elmira), and [Syracuse](/wiki/Syracuse,_New_York "Syracuse, New York"), [Albany](/wiki/Albany,_New_York "Albany, New York") and [Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania") (at Binghamton). A somewhat-unorganized network of municipally-operated public transportation services operate local and limited intercity bus services between Salamanca and Elmira.

Until the demise of long-distance passenger rail service in the United States in the 1950s and 1960s, the [Erie Railroad](/wiki/Erie_Railroad "Erie Railroad") operated passenger trains in the region, with [Chicago, Illinois](/wiki/Chicago,_Illinois "Chicago, Illinois") as the western terminus and [Jersey City, New Jersey](/wiki/Jersey_City,_New_Jersey "Jersey City, New Jersey") as the eastern terminus, with [ferry](/wiki/Ferry "Ferry") connections to [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"). The last Erie Lackawanna passenger train, the "Lake Cities", ran on January 6, 1970.

[Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") currently does not serve the area. Proposals for [high-speed rail in New York](/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_New_York "High-speed rail in New York") have included a route from Binghamton to New York City through [Scranton, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Scranton,_Pennsylvania "Scranton, Pennsylvania"): a route that could at least partially be upgraded for high-speed rail. As of 2011, the highest priority for high-speed rail projects in New York is in the [Empire Corridor](/wiki/Empire_Corridor "Empire Corridor"), of which no part crosses the Southern Tier. The hilly terrain of the Southern Tier's I-86 corridor is not ideal for high-speed rail service, especially compared to the relatively flat and straight land in the Empire Corridor.

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Economy")]

Government services are the largest employer in the area. Of second and declining importance is manufacturing. The region's manufacturing economy has suffered for decades, but factories are found in the region's larger communities. Fortune 500 materials maker [Corning Inc.](/wiki/Corning_Inc. "Corning Inc.") is headquartered in Steuben County. Broome County has a large high-tech industry and is the birthplace of [IBM](/wiki/IBM "IBM") and [flight simulation](/wiki/Flight_simulation "Flight simulation"). In addition, other factories in the region make military aircraft, televisions, furniture, metal forgings and machine tools.

The area includes the northern extent of the [Marcellus Formation](/wiki/Marcellus_Formation "Marcellus Formation") and natural gas. Crude oil and oil sands continue to be extracted from Southern Tier wells as they have for over a century.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5) There is significant debate about allowing [hydraulic fracturing](/wiki/Hydraulic_fracturing "Hydraulic fracturing") of the Marcellus Shale in the Southern Tier, which is currently banned in New York.

Cummins engine company has a large production facility located in Jamestown, NY. The Jamestown Engine Plant, established in 1974, is one of the top five heavy-duty diesel engine producers worldwide with production in recent years typically exceeding 100,000 engines annually. The JEP also remains one of the company's largest manufacturing facilities, as it accounts for 12 percent of Cummins' total engine production in 2012.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

[Agriculture](/wiki/Agriculture "Agriculture") is also a major part of the economy. Leading products are dairy, vegetables, orchard fruit and wine grapes (the last of which typically grows only on the fringes of the Southern Tier, as the inland areas tend to not have a long enough growing season to support it). In addition, two prominent [microbreweries](/wiki/Microbrewery "Microbrewery"), the [Southern Tier Brewing Company](/wiki/Southern_Tier_Brewing_Company "Southern Tier Brewing Company") in [Lakewood, New York](/wiki/Lakewood,_New_York "Lakewood, New York") and the Ellicottville Brewing Company in [Ellicottville](/wiki/Ellicottville_(village),_New_York "Ellicottville (village), New York"), operate in the western Southern Tier.

The western and northern edges of the Southern Tier are known as [ski country](/wiki/Ski_country "Ski country"), and the hilly terrain (that forms a [continental divide](/wiki/Continental_divide "Continental divide") known as the Chautauqua Ridge) is notorious for frequent and heavy [lake effect snow](/wiki/Lake_effect_snow "Lake effect snow"). As a result, [Ellicottville](/wiki/Ellicottville_(village),_New_York "Ellicottville (village), New York") has become a "ski town" with both the Holimont and [Holiday Valley](/wiki/Holiday_Valley_(ski_resort) "Holiday Valley (ski resort)") resorts in the vicinity; the two resorts draw numerous tourists, particularly from [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"), for which [U.S. Route 219](/wiki/U.S._Route_219 "U.S. Route 219") provides easy access. At its peak in the 1960s, over a dozen ski resorts resided in the Southern Tier, many in Cattaraugus County, before most of them closed due to various assorted causes by the 1980s.

## Culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Culture")]

The Southern Tier is home to two professional symphony orchestras: the [Binghamton Philharmonic](/wiki/Binghamton_Philharmonic "Binghamton Philharmonic") in Binghamton and the [Orchestra of the Southern Finger Lakes](/wiki/Orchestra_of_the_Southern_Finger_Lakes "Orchestra of the Southern Finger Lakes") in Corning. Binghamton also has the Binghamton Youth Symphony and the Binghamton Community Orchestra.

The [Tri-Cities Opera Company](/wiki/Tri-Cities_Opera_Company "Tri-Cities Opera Company") present full-scale operas in Binghamton.

## Media and entertainment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Southern_Tier&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Media and entertainment")]

Most of the Southern Tier is either served by the Elmira-Corning television market or the Binghamton television market. Cattaraugus and Allegany Counties are out of these stations' ranges, however, and are instead served by the Buffalo and Erie television markets. Two stations (more-or-less independent [WVTT-CD](/wiki/WVTT-CD "WVTT-CD") and [Retro Television Network](/wiki/Retro_Television_Network "Retro Television Network") owned-and-operated translator [WBUO-LD](/wiki/WBUO-LD "WBUO-LD")) are licensed to Olean but serve Buffalo in practice.

The Olean, Elmira-Corning, and Binghamton radio markets directly serve the Southern Tier, and the Ithaca market indirectly serves some of the area. Seven Mountains Media is the dominant broadcaster in Olean and Elmira-Corning. [iHeartMedia](/wiki/IHeartMedia "IHeartMedia") and [Cumulus Media](/wiki/Cumulus_Media "Cumulus Media") own station clusters only in Binghamton.

Notable newspapers include The Leader of Corning, the Elmira [Star-Gazette](/wiki/Star_Gazette "Star Gazette"), the Binghamton [Press & Sun-Bulletin](/wiki/Press_%26_Sun-Bulletin "Press & Sun-Bulletin"), Hornell Evening Tribune, the Wellsville Daily Reporter, the [Olean Times Herald](/wiki/Olean_Times_Herald "Olean Times Herald"), the [Salamanca Press](/wiki/Salamanca_Press "Salamanca Press"), [The Post-Journal](/wiki/The_Post-Journal "The Post-Journal") of Jamestown, and [The Observer](/wiki/Observer_(Dunkirk) "Observer (Dunkirk)") of Dunkirk. The Tribune, Reporter and Leader are all owned by [GateHouse Media](/wiki/GateHouse_Media "GateHouse Media"); [Gannett Company](/wiki/Gannett_Company "Gannett Company") owns the Star-Gazette and Press & Sun-Bulletin.

The Southern Tier has an eclectic history of sports. From 1979 to 2009 the [LPGA Corning Classic](/wiki/LPGA_Corning_Classic "LPGA Corning Classic") was held at the Corning Country Club. Endicott's En-Joie Golf Course hosted a PGA Tour event, the [B.C. Open](/wiki/B.C._Open "B.C. Open"), from 1971 until 2006. It then became the [Dick's Sporting Goods Open](/wiki/Dick%27s_Sporting_Goods_Open "Dick's Sporting Goods Open") which is a [PGA Tour Champions](/wiki/PGA_Tour_Champions "PGA Tour Champions") event still played each summer.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Binghamton has a AA baseball team, the [Binghamton Rumble Ponies](/wiki/Binghamton_Rumble_Ponies "Binghamton Rumble Ponies"), and a [Federal Prospects Hockey League](/wiki/Federal_Prospects_Hockey_League "Federal Prospects Hockey League") franchise, the [Binghamton Black Bears](/wiki/Binghamton_Black_Bears "Binghamton Black Bears"). Depending on the boundary definition, [Watkins Glen International Speedway](/wiki/Watkins_Glen_International_Speedway "Watkins Glen International Speedway"), a [NASCAR](/wiki/NASCAR "NASCAR") and [Indy Racing League](/wiki/Indy_Racing_League "Indy Racing League") sanctioned [road racing](/wiki/Road_racing "Road racing") track, is located in the Southern Tier region.

From 2000 to 2017, Elmira had a professional ice hockey team in the [Elmira Jackals](/wiki/Elmira_Jackals "Elmira Jackals"). They played in the [United Hockey League](/wiki/United_Hockey_League "United Hockey League") from 2000 to 2007 and the [ECHL](/wiki/ECHL "ECHL") for the franchise's remaining years. The Jackals were replaced by a new [Federal Hockey League](/wiki/Federal_Hockey_League "Federal Hockey League") franchise called the [Elmira Enforcers](/wiki/Elmira_Enforcers "Elmira Enforcers") from 2018 to 2021.[[8]](#cite_note-8) The last [minor league baseball](/wiki/Minor_league_baseball "Minor league baseball") team in the Southern Tier west of Binghamton, the [Jamestown Jammers](/wiki/Jamestown_Jammers "Jamestown Jammers"), relocated out of the area in 2016, ending an era in which the [New York–Penn League](/wiki/New_York%E2%80%93Penn_League "New York–Penn League") featured pro teams in Jamestown, Olean, Wellsville, Hornell, Corning, Elmira and Oneonta over the course of its history; [collegiate summer baseball](/wiki/Collegiate_summer_baseball "Collegiate summer baseball") still thrives in the region in the form of the [New York Collegiate Baseball League](/wiki/New_York_Collegiate_Baseball_League "New York Collegiate Baseball League") and [Perfect Game Collegiate Baseball League](/wiki/Perfect_Game_Collegiate_Baseball_League "Perfect Game Collegiate Baseball League"), both with multiple teams in the region, and [Town Team Baseball](/wiki/Town_Team_Baseball "Town Team Baseball") also operated in the form of the Southwestern New York Men's Baseball League from 2014 to 2016. Only one major league franchise has ever resided in the Southern Tier: the professional basketball team [Elmira Colonels](/wiki/Elmira_Colonels "Elmira Colonels"), which played from 1952 to 1953.
